Tutorial 4: Learning From Others (Interactive Notebook)

Social learning strategies and the power of conformity

  • How to use this notebook
  • Word list（単語リスト）
  • Why copy others?
  • Setup
  • How should you use the majority?
  • A society of learners
  • Does copying help?
  • Try it! — explore the society
  • The big idea
  • Optional appendix: agents can be different
  • Group-project seed: a mixed society
  • What you learned

How to use this notebook

Open ScienceHighSchool.Rproj first. Run each code block with the green Run button, or place your cursor in the code and press Cmd/Ctrl + Enter.

Change one small thing at a time. Then run the block again and look at the result.

Word list（単語リスト）

English 日本語 Meaning
social learning 社会学習 learning by observing others
individual learning 個人学習 learning from your own experience
conformity 同調 following the majority more than proportionally
strategy 戦略 a rule for deciding what to do
majority 多数派 the option most people chose

Why copy others?

You arrive in a new town, hungry. Two ramen shops: one has a queue, one is empty. Where do you go?

Most people join the queue — and that is often smart. Other people’s choices contain information: they may know something you don’t. Humans are the champions of social learning; it is the foundation of culture, science, and TikTok trends alike.

But copying has a dark side, which we will discover in Tutorial 5. First, let’s give our agents the ability to watch each other.

Setup

Load the model functions from R/bandit_functions.R. Tutorial 3 introduced softmax() and simulate_learner(). Today we will meet the new group functions in the same file.

source("R/bandit_functions.R")
library(ggplot2)
set.seed(2026)

How should you use the majority?

Suppose 10 agents played last round: 7 chose B, 3 chose A. You get to peek before choosing. How strongly should the majority pull you?

One flexible rule: choose option \(k\) with probability proportional to (number of people choosing \(k\)) raised to a power \(\theta\) (theta):

\[ p(\text{copy option } k) \propto (\text{count}_k)^{\,\theta} \]

social_weights() is a new helper function. In R, typing a function’s name without () shows its recipe instead of running it:

social_weights
function (counts, theta = 1) 
{
    w <- (counts + 0.1)^theta
    w/sum(w)
}

The first line of the recipe raises each count to the power \(\theta\) (theta). The last line turns the weights into probabilities that add up to 1. The small + 0.1 keeps an option possible even when nobody chose it last time.

The exponent \(\theta\) is the personality of the copier:

counts <- c(3, 7)   # A was chosen 3 times, B was chosen 7 times
social_weights(counts, theta = 0)   # ignore popularity completely
[1] 0.5 0.5
social_weights(counts, theta = 1)   # copy in proportion: 30% / 70%
[1] 0.3039216 0.6960784
social_weights(counts, theta = 3)   # CONFORMITY: majority is exaggerated!
[1] 0.07683994 0.92316006

With \(\theta = 3\), a 70% majority attracts about 92% of copiers. Following the majority more than proportionally is called conformist-biased copying, and experiments show humans (and even fish and some ants) often do exactly this.

grid <- expand.grid(share = seq(0.05, 0.95, by = 0.05),
                    theta = c(0.5, 1, 3))
grid$p_copy <- mapply(function(s, th) social_weights(c(10 * s, 10 * (1 - s)), th)[1],
                      grid$share, grid$theta)
ggplot(grid, aes(share, p_copy, color = factor(theta))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dotted") +
  labs(title = "How theta changes copying",
       x = "share of group choosing option A",
       y = "probability of copying A",
       color = expression(theta)) +
  coord_equal()

The dotted line is proportional copying. Above it (steep S-shape, \(\theta=3\)): conformity. Below it (\(\theta=0.5\)): the majority is weakened.

A society of learners

Now the main event. We put many agents in the same world. Each agent mixes two sources of information:

  • its own experience (the Q values from Tutorial 3), and
  • what the group did on the previous round (the social weights above).

One number, \(\sigma\) (sigma, from 0 to 1), sets the mix:

\[ p(\text{choice}) = (1 - \sigma) \times p_{\text{individual}} \;+\; \sigma \times p_{\text{social}} \]

  • \(\sigma = 0\): pure individual learner — ignores everybody
  • \(\sigma = 1\): pure copier — never uses its own experience
  • in between: a bit of both (like most of us!)

The function simulate_group() in R/bandit_functions.R implements this. Open the file and read it — you already know every ingredient!

We use a difficult world here. Option A pays 30% of the time. Option B pays 50% of the time. The small difference makes it hard for one agent to discover which option is better.

sim <- simulate_group(n_agents = 10, n_trials = 100,
                      reward_probs = c(0.3, 0.5),
                      alpha = 0.3, beta = 5,
                      sigma = 0.3, theta = 2)
head(sim)

Each row = one agent on one trial. Let’s watch the whole group at once:

ggplot(sim, aes(x = trial, y = agent, fill = factor(choice))) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("1" = "grey80", "2" = "seagreen"),
                    name = "choice", labels = c("A (30%)", "B (50%)")) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 1:10) +
  labs(title = "10 agents learning together (green = better option)")

Green spreads through the group like a rumour. That is culture, in miniature.

Does copying help?

Let’s compare societies with different amounts of social learning. average_performance() runs the simulation many times and reports, for each trial, the fraction of agents choosing the better option:

mix <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c(0, 0.3, 0.6), function(s) {
  perf <- average_performance(n_reps = 30, n_agents = 10, n_trials = 100,
                              reward_probs = c(0.3, 0.5),
                              alpha = 0.3, beta = 5,
                              sigma = s, theta = 2)
  transform(perf, sigma = s)
}))

ggplot(mix, aes(trial, correct, color = factor(sigma))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  ylim(0, 1) +
  labs(title = "Is a copying society a smarter society?",
       y = "fraction choosing the better option",
       color = expression(sigma))

Look carefully: societies with moderate copying (\(\sigma = 0.3\)–\(0.6\)) typically end up better than pure individual learners. Copying spreads good discoveries quickly, and the majority filters out individual mistakes. Group intelligence for free!

…or is it? (Cliffhanger for Tutorial 5.)

Try it! — explore the society

  1. Big city vs small village: run the comparison with n_agents = 3 and n_agents = 30. Does group size change how much copying helps?
  2. Weak majority or minority seeking? Set theta = 0.5 first. This still favours the majority, but weakly. Then set theta = -1, which favours the minority. How does each society perform?
  3. The pure-copier trap: try sigma = 0.95. Watch the tile plot from earlier — what goes wrong? Can a group learn if almost nobody collects their own information?

The big idea

\(\sigma\) (how much to copy) and \(\theta\) (how strongly to follow majorities) are social learning strategies. Evolution, development, and culture all tune these knobs. A central question in current research — including at RIKEN and in the COSMOS summer school — is: which strategies are smart, and when? Later today, your group will investigate part of that question.

Optional appendix: agents can be different

So far, every agent has had the same \(\alpha\) (alpha), \(\beta\) (beta), \(\sigma\) (sigma), and \(\theta\) (theta). Real people have individual differences（個人差）, also called heterogeneity.

simulate_group() can take one value per agent for any of these parameters. First, here are different individual-learning parameters:

alpha_by_agent <- rep(c(0.1, 0.7), each = 6)
beta_by_agent <- rep(c(2, 8), each = 3, times = 2)

set.seed(2026)
mixed_agents <- simulate_group(
  n_agents = 12, n_trials = 100, reward_probs = c(0.3, 0.5),
  alpha = alpha_by_agent, beta = beta_by_agent,
  sigma = 0.3, theta = 2)
aggregate(reward ~ alpha + beta, data = mixed_agents, FUN = mean)

Here, agents have different combinations of learning speed and exploration. Predict which type will earn more. One run is noisy, so repeat the simulation many times before you make a conclusion.

Group-project seed: a mixed society

What if eight agents balance individual and social learning, but two agents are strong conformists? Predict before running: can two conformists pull the whole group toward a herd?

sigma_by_agent <- c(rep(0.4, 8), rep(0.9, 2))
theta_by_agent <- c(rep(1, 8), rep(4, 2))

set.seed(2026)
mixed_social <- simulate_group(
  n_agents = 10, n_trials = 150, reward_probs = c(0.3, 0.5),
  sigma = sigma_by_agent, theta = theta_by_agent, change_point = 76)
ggplot(mixed_social, aes(trial, agent, fill = factor(choice))) +
  geom_tile() + geom_vline(xintercept = 75.5, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Eight balanced learners + two strong conformists")

Compare this mixed society with a society where everyone has sigma = 0.4 and theta = 1. Try changing the number of conformists. Does your answer change in a stable world (change_point = NA)? This could become your group project.

What you learned

  • Other people’s behaviour is information; copying is often rational
  • \(\theta\): proportional imitation vs conformity (exaggerating majorities)
  • \(\sigma\): the balance between own experience and social information
  • Moderate copying can make the whole group smarter

Next tutorial: when crowds are wise, when they go mad — and how to tell the difference.
